Title: Enzymatic Product Formation Impairs Both the Chloroplast Receptor-binding Function as Well as Translocation Competence of the NADPH: Protochlorophyllide Oxidoreductase, a Nuclear-encoded Plastid Precursor Protein

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Light-dependent NADPH:protochlorophyllide oxidoreductase; short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase family
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Light-dependent reduction of protochlorophyllide to chlorophyllide using NADPH
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate chlorophyll precursor/product in the chlorophyll branch
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key enzyme of chlorophyll biosynthesis in higher plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Barley precursor POR (pPOR) synthesized in vitro is enzymatically active and converts protochlorophyllide to chlorophyllide in a light- and NADPH-dependent manner. 2. Substrate-bound pPOR complexes (pPOR-PChlide and pPOR-PChlide-NADPH) retain chloroplast envelope binding and can be imported, whereas enzymatically generated chlorophyllide remains tightly but noncovalently bound and blocks receptor binding and transport. 3. When chloroplast-bound pPOR-PChlide-NADPH is illuminated, nascent chlorophyllide slows translocation and causes transient 41- and 39-kD import intermediates; ongoing translocation driven by intraplastidic PChlide can partially overcome these blocks.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enzymatic formation of chlorophyllide by pPOR impairs chloroplast receptor binding and reduces translocation competence of the POR precursor, whereas substrate binding alone does not prevent import.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro transcription/translation from full-length barley cDNA; pigment binding and fluorescence spectroscopy; light/dark enzyme assays; chloroplast/etioplast import assays; thermolysin protection; trypsin sensitivity; in vitro leader peptidase processing
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immediate chlorophyll precursor required for photosynthetic pigment formation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Studied as a nuclear-encoded plastid precursor in chloroplasts and etioplasts of 5-day-old seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Precursor synthesized in cytosol; imported into plastids/chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light versus dark incubation; exogenous protochlorophyllide; NADPH; chlorophyllide; ALA feeding to raise intraplastidic protochlorophyllide; ATP depletion/addition
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant for engineering chlorophyll biosynthesis only if precursor import competence is preserved; avoid designs that promote premature catalytic product formation before plastid import
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Premature product formation creates a transport-incompetent folded precursor and may reduce plastid targeting efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper focuses on precursor import competence rather than whole-plant trait engineering. The active species experimentally analyzed is the precursor pPOR produced from barley cDNA clone A7. Exogenously added chlorophyllide does not inhibit import, indicating that inhibition requires chlorophyllide generated on and retained by pPOR.
